title: Probabilistic size-and-shape functional mixed models

reviews.summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.
reviews.strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.
reviews.weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.
reviews.questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.
reviews.limitations: The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.

Strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.

Weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.

Questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.

Limitations:
The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Rating:
7
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper presents a Bayesian functional mixed‐effects framework for estimating a population‐level function μ under both amplitude and unrestricted phase variability. Observed curves are modeled as D_{γ_i}(μ + v_i + ε_i), where γ_i acts via a norm‐preserving diffeomorphic warp on L², v_i captures amplitude deviations, and ε_i is Gaussian noise. Two priors for phase functions are proposed—a one‐parameter quadratic family (PM1) and a finite‐dimensional Dirichlet‐based family (PM2)—and functions are represented in finite bases (e.g., B‐splines or data‐driven fPCA). Posterior inference is carried out via MCMC that leverages the group structure for efficient exploration. The methodology is evaluated on simulations and multiple real datasets (growth curves, ECG PQRST complexes, and others), with comparisons to the frequentist warpMix implementation and a basic Bayesian baseline.

Strengths:  
1. Novelty and Positioning: By focusing on the L² norm‐preserving action of the diffeomorphism group, the paper offers a fresh Bayesian perspective on phase‐amplitude separation. This geometric “size‐and‐shape” viewpoint extends beyond value‐preserving models and fills a clear methodological gap in functional data analysis.  
2. Clarity of Intuition: The paper provides illuminating mathematical and visual explanations of value‐preserving versus norm‐preserving warps, supported by concrete examples. The historical motivation drawn from 2D shape analysis situates the work well.  
3. Practical Implementation: The authors detail how to approximate infinite‐dimensional operations via basis projections (e.g., B‐splines), with supplementary experiments in the appendix showing alternatives (functional PCA) and their trade‐offs.  
4. Empirical Evaluation: Extensive simulations, including data generated under competing models, and multiple real‐data case studies demonstrate that the posterior mean under the proposed model recovers sharper geometric features than warpMix. Replicability is aided by thorough appendices.  
5. Bayesian Regularization: Informative priors on phase functions (both PM1 and PM2) and priors on variance components effectively regularize the inference, yielding uncertainty quantification unavailable in most frequentist registration‐then‐estimate pipelines.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:  
1. Comparative Evaluation: Current benchmarks are limited to the default warpMix implementation. At minimum, the authors should align the noise‐variance assumptions—warpMix’s default σ²≈10⁻³ versus the proposed model’s σ²≈1 and σ_c²≈0.25—to ensure fair comparisons. More importantly, comparisons to related approaches by Kurtek, Srivastava et al., Raket et al. (2014), and Cheng et al. (2016), which also address norm‐preserving registration or shape‐based mean estimation, would strengthen empirical claims.  
2. Downstream Tasks: Beyond visual assessments, it would be valuable to demonstrate utility in a quantifiable downstream task (e.g., classification or forecasting) to show that sharper feature recovery translates into practical gains.  
3. Theoretical Identifiability: The paper currently lacks formal identifiability results for μ’s size‐and‐shape under the unrestricted diffeomorphism group. Providing conditions or a sketch of identifiability (e.g., under which group‐action equivalence classes μ can be uniquely recovered) would bolster the theoretical contribution.  
4. Computational Scalability: MCMC runtimes grow substantially with sample size n, curve resolution T, and basis dimensions B_f, B_r. The authors might explore variational approximations or Hamiltonian Monte Carlo to reduce computational cost and assess the trade‐off between speed and accuracy. Guidance on MCMC diagnostics and mixing for large T or n would also aid practitioners.  
5. Hyperparameter and Basis Selection: While the paper includes a limited sensitivity study on θ_γ and basis truncation, more systematic guidance—such as cross‐validation, marginal‐likelihood criteria, or reversible‐jump schemes—for choosing B_f, B_r, and prior precisions would improve robustness in new applications.  
6. Model Extensions and Robustness: The current model assumes common uniform discretization and iid Gaussian noise. Discussion or preliminary results on adapting to irregular or sparse sampling, heteroskedastic or dependent errors, and non‐Gaussian noise scenarios would clarify practical applicability.

Additional Questions:  
1. How sensitive are posterior estimates to the choice of basis family (e.g., modified Fourier vs. B‐splines) when μ exhibits both global and local features?  
2. Can the Dirichlet precision θ_γ and one‐parameter prior in PM1 be adapted via hyperpriors to improve uncertainty quantification?  
3. Under what conditions (e.g., smoothness of μ or regularity of γ_i) does identifiability of the size‐and‐shape representative hold?  
4. How would the framework need to change to accommodate multivariate functional data or manifold‐valued observations?  
5. Can faster approximate inference methods (e.g., variational inference) achieve comparable accuracy to MCMC, especially for large‐scale datasets?

Limitations (Acknowledged):  
Section 3 outlines key modeling assumptions—fixed grids, Gaussian errors, and basis truncation—and discusses some extensions. However, deeper examination of posterior consistency under model misspecification and overfitting risks in PM2 (with large T_γ) is warranted.

Rating: 7 (Accept)  
This work makes a meaningful methodological advance in Bayesian functional data analysis by integrating geometric group actions with mixed‐effects modeling. Addressing the above points—especially fairer comparisons, downstream evaluations, and guidance on scalability and identifiability—would further strengthen an already solid contribution.

reviews.summary: The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.
reviews.strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.
reviews.weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.
reviews.questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?
reviews.limitations: See above
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.

Strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:
## Significance:

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.

Questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?

Limitations:
See above

Rating:
7

reviews.summary: The paper considers uncertainty quantification for one-dimensional regression tasks, where the observations are noisy. A rather advanced additive model considering invariances under space-time unitary transformations is proposted. Numerical experiments demonstrate the superiority of the approach over other state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.strengths:
- Due to the strong modeling assumptions, on synthethic datasets, the method can recover the true signal even in situations where there is lots of noise and classical methods (such as GPs) may fail. 
- The methods seems to be useful to analyze the Berkley growth spurt data set.
reviews.weaknesses:
- It is unclear whether the work will be of interest to the wider machine learning audience and the considered topic and datasets feel more of a niche. 
- Despite the well-written introduction, the paper was quite difficult to understand due to the advanced math and notations -- after reading it I am still quite unsure why I should really care about functional mixed models and their Bayesian inference.
reviews.questions: I am slightly puzzled as of why NeurIPS was chosen as a venue for this paper - the work may not get the appreciation here it deserves. reviews.text:
Summary  
This manuscript develops a Bayesian functional mixed‐effects model to recover a population‐level function μ in the presence of both amplitude variability and unrestricted phase (warping) variability. The key innovation is to treat phase warping as a norm‐preserving (unitary) action of the diffeomorphism group on an L² Hilbert space, inducing rotations of a chosen orthonormal basis. Two prior formulations on the warping group are proposed—a one‐parameter quadratic family and a finite‐dimensional Dirichlet model—and posterior inference is carried out via Metropolis–Hastings MCMC that exploits the group geometry. Through extensive simulations and applications to synthetic data, the Berkeley growth‐spurt dataset, ECG PQRST complexes, and additional functional datasets, the authors demonstrate improved recovery of geometric (size‐and‐shape) features of μ relative to state‐of‐the‐art registration and mixed‐model baselines.

Strengths  
• Novel Geometric Perspective: By focusing on size‐and‐shape invariants under norm‐preserving warping, the paper offers a fresh Bayesian treatment of phase‐amplitude separation that goes beyond value‐preserving registration. Related work in shape analysis and functional mixed models is cited comprehensively (Alternative Reviews 1–4).  
• Technical Rigor: The derivations of marginal likelihoods, unitary‐operator priors, and Metropolis–Hastings ratios are detailed and leverage the underlying group structure. Informative priors on phase functions regularize inference in high‐noise regimes, allowing recovery of true signals where classical Gaussian process or warp‐then‐model methods can fail (Main Review Strength 1; AR1 Quality).  
• Empirical Performance: Numerical studies on both synthetic benchmarks—where true warping is known—and multiple real datasets (growth curves, ECGs, etc.) consistently show that the posterior mean under the proposed model better captures geometric features than warpMix and other frequentist/Bayesian competitors (AR1–AR4). Sensitivity analyses in the appendices suggest robustness to basis‐size over‐specification.

Weaknesses  
• Audience and Venue Fit: It remains unclear whether this highly specialized Bayesian‐shape approach will appeal broadly to the machine learning community. The focus on functional mixed models and specialized statistical theory may be more suited to a dedicated statistics journal than NeurIPS (Main Review Weakness 1; Main Review Question).  
• Clarity and Accessibility: While the introduction is well‐written, the body of the paper is dense with advanced notation and group‐action formalism. Many readers may find the mathematical presentation challenging, and key intuitions about why practitioners should care are not highlighted early (Main Review Weakness 2; AR1 & AR2 Clarity). A schematic overview or workflow diagram in the main text could improve readability.  
• Theoretical Guarantees: No formal identifiability or posterior consistency results are provided, despite the centrality of unique size‐and‐shape recovery under nonparametric warping. Conditions under which μ’s size‐and‐shape are identifiable should be clarified (AR1, AR2, AR4 questions).  
• Computational Scalability: The MCMC sampler, while geometrically informed, incurs substantial runtime (hundreds of minutes for moderate sample sizes and discretization grids). This limits applicability to large n or high‐resolution functional data. The paper mentions but does not explore faster inference alternatives (e.g., variational methods or Hamiltonian Monte Carlo) (AR1–AR4 limitations).  
• Basis Selection and Hyperparameters: The need to pre‐specify basis dimensions (B_f, B_r) and warping‐prior precision (θ_γ) is handled via ad hoc guidelines. More systematic model‐selection criteria (e.g., cross‐validation or marginal‐likelihood estimates) or data‐driven basis adaptation (reversible‐jump MCMC or hyperpriors) would strengthen practical applicability (AR2–AR3 suggestions).  
• Limited Scope of Comparisons: Empirical evaluation focuses on warpMix and a small set of baselines. Including comparisons to other recent Bayesian registration or square‐root velocity methods (e.g., Raket et al., Cheng et al.) would better situate the contributions (AR2 & AR3 questions).  
• Irregular and Sparse Sampling: The current framework assumes dense, uniform discretization on a common grid and iid Gaussian noise. Extensions to irregularly or sparsely sampled functions, or to heteroskedastic/dependent errors, are mentioned only in passing and merit deeper discussion (AR1–AR4 limitations, AR2 & AR3 questions).

Questions and Suggestions  
1. Identifiability: Under what conditions on the warping group and priors is μ’s size‐and‐shape uniquely identifiable? Can the authors formalize these conditions or provide partial guarantees?  
2. Inference Scalability: Have you considered variational approximations or Hamiltonian Monte Carlo to reduce MCMC runtime? How do these alternatives affect accuracy and mixing for large T or n?  
3. Basis and Hyperparameter Selection: Can you recommend practical criteria (e.g., cross‐validation scores, marginal likelihoods) for choosing B_f, B_r, and θ_γ? Would a hyperprior on these quantities improve adaptivity?  
4. Noise and Sampling Assumptions: How sensitive are posterior estimates and credible intervals to violations of iid Gaussian noise—such as heteroskedastic or dependent errors? What modifications would be needed for irregularly sampled or sparse functional data?  
5. Broader Comparisons: To strengthen empirical claims, could you benchmark against additional Bayesian registration methods (e.g., square‐root velocity Gaussian processes) and more varied real datasets beyond growth curves and ECGs?  
6. Presentation: A high‐level schematic in the main text outlining model construction, inference steps, and practical guidelines would greatly aid readers new to geometric functional data analysis.

Decision and Rating: 5 (Borderline Reject)  
While this work offers a novel and technically sound framework for Bayesian size‐and‐shape recovery under nonparametric warping, its narrow methodological focus, accessibility challenges, lack of theoretical identifiability guarantees, and high computational cost raise concerns about its suitability for NeurIPS. I encourage the authors to address the above questions and consider reframing the narrative toward broader applicability or targeting a specialized statistics venue.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This manuscript tackles the challenging problem of reliably recovering a population‐level fixed effect function μ in a functional mixed‐effects framework with both amplitude and unrestricted phase variability. Building on the norm‐preserving action of the diffeomorphism group Γ in L², the authors model observations as yi(t)=D_{γi}(μ+v_i)(t)+ε_i(t) without assuming a finite‐rank error covariance. They derive Metropolis–Hastings updates for sampling from the joint posterior of μ, subject‐specific phase functions γ_i, amplitude deviations v_i, and noise parameters, and illustrate convergence through both synthetic and real‐data experiments (growth curves, ECG complexes, and other functional datasets).

Strengths:  
1. Originality and Modeling Innovation:  
   • By leveraging a group‐action viewpoint—treating phase warping as unitary rotations in Hilbert space—the paper extends beyond standard value‐preserving registration approaches, directly targeting the “size‐and‐shape” invariant of μ.  
   • The two proposed priors for γ_i (the one‐parameter quadratic family and the finite‐dimensional Dirichlet‐based model) are well motivated by the underlying geometry of Γ.  

2. Theoretical and Algorithmic Contributions:  
   • The detailed derivation of the marginal likelihood and the Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratios demonstrates technical rigor.  
   • Informative priors on phase functions regularize inference and exploit group structure for efficient exploration of an infinite‐dimensional warping space.  

3. Empirical Validation:  
   • Extensive simulations—including data generated under competing warpMix and other Bayesian baselines—and multiple real‐world applications showcase that the posterior mean of μ more accurately captures geometric features compared to state‐of‐the‐art alternatives.  
   • The convergence analysis through traceplots and diagnostics in the appendices provides practical insights into the sampler’s behavior.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:  
1. Identifiability and Theoretical Guarantees:  
   • Neither formal identifiability conditions for μ’s size‐and‐shape nor posterior contraction or consistency results are provided. As Alternative Reviews 1 and 4 note, characterizing the regimes under which μ is uniquely recoverable would strengthen the theoretical foundation.  

2. Prior and Model Sensitivity:  
   • The choice of prior precision θ_γ in the nonparametric phase model can substantially affect posterior estimates. A more thorough sensitivity analysis—beyond the limited study—would guide practitioners.  
   • Guidance on selecting basis truncation dimensions B_f and B_r is currently ad hoc. Incorporating model‐selection strategies (e.g., cross‐validation, reversible‐jump MCMC, or marginal likelihood criteria) could improve robustness, as suggested in Alternative Reviews 1 and 2.

3. Computational Scalability:  
   • MCMC runtimes grow quickly with sample size n, grid resolution T, and basis dimensions, reaching hundreds of minutes in some experiments. Exploring faster approximations, such as variational inference or Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (Alternative Reviews 2 and 3), may greatly enhance scalability.  
   • Clear recommendations on practical MCMC diagnostics and mixing for large T or n—beyond the current appendices—would be valuable for users facing high‐dimensional data.

4. Applicability to Irregular or Dependent Data:  
   • The method assumes common regular discretization and iid Gaussian noise. Real‐world functional data are often irregularly or sparsely sampled and may exhibit heteroskedastic or correlated errors. Discussing extensions to handle missing observations or non‐Gaussian noise (raised in Alternative Reviews 1, 2, and 4) would broaden the approach’s applicability.

5. Clarity and Presentation:  
   • While the manuscript is mathematically precise, the dense notation and lengthy technical appendices can overwhelm readers. A schematic workflow or conceptual diagram in the main text could improve accessibility, in line with suggestions from Alternative Reviews 1 and 2.

Questions for the Authors:  
1. Under what conditions on Γ and the error process can one guarantee identifiability of μ’s size‐and‐shape representative?  
2. How would the proposed framework adapt to irregularly or sparsely sampled functional data? What modifications to priors on Γ or discretization would be required?  
3. Can you provide practical criteria (e.g., cross‐validation or marginal‐likelihood comparisons) for selecting basis sizes B_f and B_r?  
4. Have you evaluated faster inference schemes—variational approximations or Hamiltonian Monte Carlo—and how do they compare in accuracy and runtime?  
5. How sensitive are posterior credible intervals to departures from the iid Gaussian noise assumption, such as dependent or heteroskedastic errors?  
6. Would hyperpriors on θ_γ or on basis dimensions enhance adaptivity and reduce the need for manual tuning?

Decision and Rating:  
Overall, this work addresses a significant methodological gap in functional data analysis by providing a Bayesian, geometry‐aware framework for size‐and‐shape recovery under unrestricted phase variability. The careful modeling, thorough empirical validation, and clear derivations lend credibility to the approach. However, the absence of identifiability theory, reliance on careful hyperparameter tuning, and substantial computational cost temper enthusiasm. In light of the post‐rebuttal revisions that have addressed several initial concerns, I recommend a borderline acceptance.  

Rating: 5 (Borderline)

reviews.summary: In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.
reviews.strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.
reviews.weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.
reviews.questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?
reviews.limitations: The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.

To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.

Strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.

Weaknesses:

- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.

Questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper proposes a Bayesian functional mixed‐effects model for estimating a population‐level size‐and‐shape function μ under both amplitude and unrestricted phase variability. The authors embed μ and individual deviations in an L² Hilbert space and use a norm‐preserving action of the infinite‐dimensional diffeomorphism group to capture phase warping as unitary rotations of a chosen basis. Two tractable priors on the warping functions are introduced—a one‐parameter quadratic family and a finite‐dimensional Dirichlet‐based model—and posterior inference is carried out via Metropolis–Hastings MCMC. Synthetic experiments and real‐data applications (e.g., growth curves and ECG PQRST complexes) demonstrate that the posterior mean under the proposed model outperforms the state‐of‐the‐art warpMix method in recovering key geometric features.

Strengths  
1. Novel geometric perspective. Building on the L² norm–preserving group action, the paper offers a fresh Bayesian approach to infer size‐and‐shape invariants beyond standard value‐preserving registration methods.  
2. Rigorous model formulation. The likelihood, prior constructions on the group Γ, and MCMC proposal mechanisms are carefully derived using the underlying Hilbert‐space geometry, and sensitivity studies show robustness to hyperparameter choices.  
3. Empirical evidence. Extensive simulations—both under the proposed model and under competing generative schemes—along with multiple real‐data case studies convincingly illustrate improved recovery of μ’s geometric features compared to warpMix.  
4. Clarity and organization. The manuscript is concise and generally well structured; most derivations are relegated to appendices, keeping the main text focused on key ideas and results.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Notation and readability. As noted in the main review, distinguishing vectors, matrices, and scalars more clearly (e.g., via boldface or typography) and consistently using symbols (such as the transpose operator) would help readers navigate the dense mathematics. A schematic workflow or figure summarizing the MCMC pipeline could also improve accessibility.  
2. Identifiability and theoretical guarantees. Neither the main review nor the empirical studies address formal identifiability conditions for the size‐and‐shape representative of μ under unrestricted phase variation. A brief discussion of when µ is recoverable (perhaps building on existing equivalence‐class results) would strengthen confidence in the method’s validity.  
3. Basis selection and dimensionality. The model requires pre‐specification of basis truncation ranks Bf and Br. Alternative reviews highlight that under‐ or over‐specification can harm inference if Bf/Br are too small, and that reversible‐jump or cross‐validation strategies could guide automated selection. The authors should discuss possible data‐driven criteria or hyperpriors to infer Bf and Br, and report sensitivity of posterior estimates to these choices.  
4. Computational cost and scalability. MCMC sampling on Γ incurs substantial runtime—especially as n, T, or basis dimensions grow—which may limit practical application on large or high‐resolution datasets. We encourage the authors to (a) report wall‐clock times for all methods, (b) explore faster alternatives (e.g., variational inference or Hamiltonian Monte Carlo on the group manifold), and (c) discuss scalability to irregular or sparsely sampled functions.  
5. Broader comparisons and failure cases. While warpMix is a relevant benchmark, comparisons to other Bayesian registration approaches (e.g., square‐root velocity GP models) or frequentist registration‐then‐model pipelines would contextualize the contribution. Additionally, reporting scenarios where the method underperforms—such as extreme noise levels or poor hyperparameter settings—would help users understand limitations.  

Questions for the Authors  
1. Under what conditions on the error structure and warping prior is the size‐and‐shape component of μ identifiable?  
2. Can Bf and Br be learned automatically via marginal likelihood, cross‐validation, or reversible‐jump MCMC? How sensitive are results to these ranks?  
3. Have you considered alternative inference strategies (e.g., variational approximations or HMC) to reduce computational burden, and if so, how do they compare in accuracy?  
4. How does the method perform with sparsely observed or irregularly sampled functional data? What changes to the prior or discretization would be required?  
5. In real‐data applications, how robust are posterior credible intervals to deviations from the iid Gaussian noise assumption (e.g., heteroskedastic or dependent errors)?  

Overall Assessment and Rating  
This work delivers an innovative Bayesian framework for functional mixed modeling under unrestricted phase variation, with solid empirical gains and a clear geometric motivation. However, it would benefit from improved notation, deeper discussion of identifiability, guidance on basis selection, expanded computational analysis, and broader comparative studies. In its current form, the manuscript is promising but requires these clarifications to reach its full potential.  

Rating: 5 (Marginal – Weak Accept)

title: Chain of Preference Optimization: Improving Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in LLMs

reviews.summary: The paper introduces Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO) to improve reasoning in LLMs. CPO utilizes preference data generated during ToT to fine-tune models. This approach improves reasoning without increasing inference complexity.
reviews.strengths:
1. The idea of CPO is interesting.
2. CPO achieves enhanced performance without increasing inference complexity.
3. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. While CPO is effective across several tasks, its performance on more complex reasoning benchmarks, such as the MATH benchmark, remains unexplored.
2. Fine-tuning with reasoning paths generated by ToT may reduce the diversity of generated paths. A systematic investigation into how the generated reasoning paths change before and after fine-tuning is needed.
3. The effectiveness of CPO is heavily dependent on the quality of reasoning paths generated by ToT. If ToT produces suboptimal paths, the preference data might not be as beneficial.
4. The paper does not adequately address the applicability of CPO to more advanced models, such as LLaMA3.
5. While CPO enhances performance, the paper lacks a deep analysis of the interpretability of the optimized reasoning paths. Understanding why certain paths are preferred could provide additional insights and improve trust in the model’s decisions.
reviews.limitations: Yes

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO), a fine-tuning framework that leverages the intermediate preference signals generated during Tree-of-Thought (ToT) search to improve Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning in large language models (LLMs). Rather than training only on the final reasoning path, CPO constructs per-step preference pairs between preferred and dispreferred reasoning steps from the ToT search tree and applies a Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) objective. As a result, the model learns to generate higher-quality reasoning chains at inference time without incurring ToT’s search overhead. Experiments on LLaMA-2 and Mistral backbones across seven datasets—spanning question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning—demonstrate an average accuracy gain of ~4.3% over standard CoT and performance on par with ToT at over 50× lower inference latency.

Strengths  
1. Novelty of Preference Supervision  
   - The paper introduces a fresh perspective by exploiting discarded intermediate thoughts in ToT as valuable supervision signals, extending beyond prior methods that only use the single best path.  
   - This per-step preference formulation addresses gradient cancellation issues in full-path DPO and represents a clear methodological advance.

2. Sound Methodology and Empirical Rigor  
   - The authors provide a principled derivation of the DPO objective and analyze why full-path supervision can lead to longest-common-prefix gradient cancellation.  
   - Experimental comparisons against strong baselines (CoT, ToT, TS-SFT) are thorough, with statistical significance testing (p < 0.01) and ablation studies on dispreferred-selection strategies, data volume, and DPO regularization.  
   - Latency measurements confirm that CPO preserves CoT-level inference speed (> 50× faster than ToT), highlighting its practical value for resource-constrained deployment.

3. Clarity and Reproducibility  
   - The manuscript is generally well organized, with clear diagrams illustrating CoT, ToT, and CPO, plus detailed descriptions of data synthesis and training procedures.  
   - Hyperparameters, compute resources, and supplemental analyses are provided, facilitating reproducibility.  

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Scope of Evaluation  
   - The paper’s evaluation is limited to question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning. Its performance on more complex benchmarks—such as the MATH datasets—or on multi-modal and open-domain tasks remains unexplored. We recommend extending experiments to at least one complex reasoning benchmark and discussing how CPO might generalize to vision-language or retrieval-augmented CoT settings.  

2. Dependence on ToT Quality and Diversity  
   - CPO’s effectiveness hinges on the quality of reasoning paths produced by ToT. Suboptimal tree search configurations (e.g., smaller branching factor k or shallow depth n) may degrade fine-tuning signals. A sensitivity analysis of ToT hyperparameters and an exploration of alternative search policies (e.g., MCTS) would clarify robustness.  
   - Fine-tuning on preference pairs may inadvertently reduce the diversity of generated reasoning paths. A systematic investigation into the shift in reasoning diversity before and after CPO—perhaps via diversity metrics or case studies—could reveal unintended biases.

3. Offline Compute Cost  
   - Generating ToT search trees for training is computationally expensive. The paper acknowledges this cost but does not quantify end-to-end GPU-hours or compare it to the inference-time savings. Providing concrete estimates of offline compute requirements would help practitioners assess trade-offs.  

4. Interpretability and Calibration  
   - While CPO aligns model outputs with higher-quality paths, the interpretability of the optimized reasoning chains is not deeply analyzed. Understanding why certain paths are preferred could improve trust and aid debugging.  
   - The use of LLM self-evaluation to score intermediate thoughts may introduce bias or miscalibration. An evaluation of scorer accuracy against human judgments and an analysis of how noisy preference labels affect downstream performance would strengthen the work.

5. Applicability to Advanced Models  
   - The paper focuses on LLaMA-2 and Mistral-7B/13B. It does not address applicability to newer or larger models (e.g., LLaMA3, 70B+), nor to multilingual settings. Empirical evidence on at least one larger backbone would substantiate claims of scalability.  

6. Societal and Safety Considerations  
   - Potential misuse—such as generating more persuasive disinformation—warrants a deeper discussion of mitigation strategies and governance (e.g., gated model release, usage monitoring).  
   - The implications of aligning models to machine-derived preferences on fairness and bias propagation should be explored, or at minimum acknowledged with suggested safeguards.

Overall Assessment  
Chain-of-Preference Optimization is a compelling and well-articulated approach that shifts expensive ToT search costs offline while preserving fast inference and boosting reasoning quality. Its novel use of intermediate ToT data for per-step preference supervision addresses key limitations of prior methods. However, to bolster its impact and facilitate real-world adoption, the paper should expand empirical evaluations to more complex and diverse settings, quantify the offline compute burden, and deepen analyses of path diversity, interpretability, and potential risks.  

Rating: 6: Weak Accept

Summary:
The paper introduces Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO) to improve reasoning in LLMs. CPO utilizes preference data generated during ToT to fine-tune models. This approach improves reasoning without increasing inference complexity.

Strengths:
1. The idea of CPO is interesting.
2. CPO achieves enhanced performance without increasing inference complexity.
3. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.

Weaknesses:
1. While CPO is effective across several tasks, its performance on more complex reasoning benchmarks, such as the MATH benchmark, remains unexplored.
2. Fine-tuning with reasoning paths generated by ToT may reduce the diversity of generated paths. A systematic investigation into how the generated reasoning paths change before and after fine-tuning is needed.
3. The effectiveness of CPO is heavily dependent on the quality of reasoning paths generated by ToT. If ToT produces suboptimal paths, the preference data might not be as beneficial.
4. The paper does not adequately address the applicability of CPO to more advanced models, such as LLaMA3.
5. While CPO enhances performance, the paper lacks a deep analysis of the interpretability of the optimized reasoning paths. Understanding why certain paths are preferred could provide additional insights and improve trust in the model’s decisions.

Limitations:
Yes

The paper presents a novel method to enhance the performance of LLMs in complex problem-solving tasks using Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO). The authors propose fine-tuning LLMs using the search tree constructed by tree-of-thought (ToT), allowing CoT to achieve similar or better results without the heavy tree-searching during inference. The CPO method aligns each step of the CoT reasoning paths with those of ToT, leveraging the inherent preference information in the tree-search process. The authors provide experimental evidence of the effectiveness of CPO, showing significant improvements in LLM performance in tasks such as question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning.

Strengths:
The paper presents a novel method, Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), that significantly enhances the CoT reasoning ability of LLMs without increasing the inference load. It offers a convincing comparison with the ToT method, demonstrating that CPO substantially reduces inference time, as evidenced by the decrease in latency from 1749s/ins to 38s/ins on Llama2-7B. Furthermore, the paper shows that CPO not only matches but surpasses the ToT baseline in terms of accuracy across multiple datasets. reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces Chain of Preference Optimization (CPO), a novel fine-tuning framework that leverages the full Tree-of-Thought (ToT) search tree to improve Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning in large language models (LLMs). By harvesting per-step preference pairs (preferred vs. dispreferred intermediate thoughts) from ToT’s pruning procedure and optimizing with a Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) objective, CPO teaches models to generate high-quality reasoning chains at inference time without incurring ToT’s considerable computational overhead. The authors evaluate CPO on multiple LLM backbones (LLaMA2-7B/13B, Mistral-7B) across seven datasets spanning question answering, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning, demonstrating an average accuracy gain of ~4.3% over standard CoT and matching or surpassing ToT performance at over 50× lower inference latency.

Strengths:
1. Novelty and Significance: CPO’s per-step preference formulation represents a clear advance over prior supervised fine-tuning approaches that only exploit final reasoning paths. By shifting the bulk of search cost to offline training while preserving CoT’s fast inference, this work offers a practical route to deploy deliberative reasoning capabilities in resource-constrained settings.
2. Methodological Rigor: The paper provides a sound theoretical motivation for using DPO and addresses gradient cancellation issues in full-path preference optimization. Detailed ablations examine dispreferred selection strategies, the impact of training data volume, and the sensitivity of per-step versus full-path supervision.
3. Empirical Breadth: Experiments cover multiple model families, seven diverse datasets, statistical significance testing (p < 0.01), and clear latency measurements (e.g., reducing Llama2-7B inference time from 1749s to 38s per instance). Comparisons against strong baselines (CoT, ToT, TS-SFT) bolster confidence in CPO’s effectiveness.
4. Clarity and Reproducibility: The manuscript is well structured, with helpful diagrams illustrating CoT, ToT, and CPO workflows. Pseudocode and appendices include implementation details, hyperparameters, and compute resources, facilitating reproducibility.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Broader Evaluation: While the focus on QA, fact verification, and arithmetic reasoning is sensible, the paper omits common benchmarks such as GSM8K and ProofWriter, as well as tasks in commonsense, code generation, or vision-language reasoning. Including results on these would strengthen claims of generality.
2. Offline Compute Cost: The ToT tree generation is computationally intensive but not quantified in terms of GPU-hours or wall-clock time. A breakdown of offline training cost versus inference savings would help practitioners assess trade-offs.
3. Hyperparameter Sensitivity: The choice of ToT search parameters (branching factor k, pruning size n) and the DPO regularization coefficient β can materially affect performance. Further ablations examining suboptimal ToT configurations, smaller k/n, and varying β would guide adoption in new domains.
4. Preference Quality and Bias: The self-evaluation scoring mechanism used during ToT may introduce evaluator bias or miscalibration. An analysis of evaluator accuracy against human judgments, as well as the robustness of CPO to noisy preference labels, is warranted.
5. Clarity on Prompts and Templates: Key details such as prompt templates and the number of evaluation samples per decision node currently reside in the appendix. Integrating these into the main text would improve accessibility.
6. Societal and Safety Considerations: The potential for adversarial or disinformation use of stronger reasoning models is noted but not deeply explored. Concrete mitigation strategies—such as gated releases, usage monitoring, or integration of human feedback—should be discussed.

Questions for the Authors:
1. LN35: How does CPO compare to existing Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)–based preference methods, and what inherent advantages does CPO confer?
2. LN117: Have you considered an online RL framework that regenerates chain-of-preferences using the updated LLM?
3. Table 1: Can you report CPO performance on additional benchmarks like GSM8K or ProofWriter?
4. How sensitive is CPO to ToT search quality when suboptimal paths are generated?
5. Can CPO be applied iteratively—i.e., regenerate preference data with the fine-tuned model—and if so, does it yield diminishing returns or overfitting?

Decision and Rating:
Weak Accept (6). CPO is a significant and well-executed contribution to efficient LLM reasoning. Addressing the points above would further strengthen its practical impact and generality.

reviews.summary:
This paper presents Chain-of-preference optimization (CPO) a self-supervised learning extension of Tree of Thought (ToT). Rather than use ToT during test time, which takes exponentially longer than end-to-end sampling, this paper proposes to use ToT at training time to annotate data for DPO fine-tuning and then use the DPO-tuned model end-to-end at test time. Notably, the DPO annotations are performed by the untuned model itself being prompted to label inferences as either useful or not. This differs from other approaches that are trained on only full successful reasoning paths.

For certain QA and reasoning tasks, the paper presents experimental evidence that CPO yields stronger models than either (1) fine-tuning the same LM on positive examples (TS-SFT) as done in previous papers or (2) running regular chain-of-thought prompting.
reviews.strengths:
1. The approach is an intuitive and straightforward improvement to multi-hop reasoning that that is much faster than tree-based inference procedures.
2. The approach is appealing as it doesn't rely on correct labels nor external LLMs other than the LLM being fine-tuned on its own preference data.
3. The methodology is well explained and easily reproducible.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. While the authors run evaluation over several datasets, they only consider 300 questions per dataset which is quite small. A stronger evaluation would use the whole test sets.
2. Details about the baseline implementation are unclear. The authors refer to both "TS-SFT" and "TS-LLM" (l.206) which in the original paper refer to different things-- SFT refers to the model fine-tuned on training examples with reasoning traces pulled from both a gold-annotated dataset and/or reasoning traces from the model that led to correct answers. However, TS-LLM in the original paper refers to the result of an iterative refinement process.
3. The choice of datasets is somewhat odd. E.g. HotpotQA is meant to be evaluated against support documents, which might explain the very low scores. They also compare against approaches (ToT, TS-SFT) that were not evaluated on any of the QA or Fact Verification tasks under consideration.  This paper did _not_ consider the Game of 24 or GSM datasets, which both ToT and TS-SFT did evaluate. 

Missing references:
1. Khalifa et al 2023: [GRACE: Discriminator-Guided Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://aclanthology.org/2023.findings-emnlp.1022.pdf)
2. Li et al 2023: [Learning Math Reasoning from Self-Sampled Correct and Partially-Correct Solutions](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=4D4TSJE6-K)
reviews.questions:
* What metrics did you use for the QA datasets? many of them use more than exact match, e.g. HotpotQA uses F1.
* The discussion around ablations in 5.3 is unclear. Are the trends consistent across different models and datasets? 
* It is odd to have only considered up to 200 instances for constructing preference pairs. Why is this experiment limited to such a small maximum? The difference between e.g. 160 and 200 seems much less important than the difference between 200 and 1000 or 1000 and 5000. 
* How many total preference pairs do you end up training on? It would be helpful to include this number in the main body of the paper.
* How stable is this approach to different prompts for state evaluation? Did you experiment with other prompts?
reviews.limitations: yes
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper presents Chain-of-preference optimization (CPO) a self-supervised learning extension of Tree of Thought (ToT). Rather than use ToT during test time, which takes exponentially longer than end-to-end sampling, this paper proposes to use ToT at training time to annotate data for DPO fine-tuning and then use the DPO-tuned model end-to-end at test time. Notably, the DPO annotations are performed by the untuned model itself being prompted to label inferences as either useful or not. This differs from other approaches that are trained on only full successful reasoning paths.

For certain QA and reasoning tasks, the paper presents experimental evidence that CPO yields stronger models than either (1) fine-tuning the same LM on positive examples (TS-SFT) as done in previous papers or (2) running regular chain-of-thought prompting.

Strengths:
1. The approach is an intuitive and straightforward improvement to multi-hop reasoning that that is much faster than tree-based inference procedures.
2. The approach is appealing as it doesn't rely on correct labels nor external LLMs other than the LLM being fine-tuned on its own preference data.
3. The methodology is well explained and easily reproducible.

Weaknesses:
1. While the authors run evaluation over several datasets, they only consider 300 questions per dataset which is quite small. A stronger evaluation would use the whole test sets.
2. Details about the baseline implementation are unclear. The authors refer to both "TS-SFT" and "TS-LLM" (l.206) which in the original paper refer to different things-- SFT refers to the model fine-tuned on training examples with reasoning traces pulled from both a gold-annotated dataset and/or reasoning traces from the model that led to correct answers. However, TS-LLM in the original paper refers to the result of an iterative refinement process.
3. The choice of datasets is somewhat odd. E.g. HotpotQA is meant to be evaluated against support documents, which might explain the very low scores. They also compare against approaches (ToT, TS-SFT) that were not evaluated on any of the QA or Fact Verification tasks under consideration.  This paper did _not_ consider the Game of 24 or GSM datasets, which both ToT and TS-SFT did evaluate. 

Missing references:
1. Khalifa et al 2023: [GRACE: Discriminator-Guided Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://aclanthology.org/2023.findings-emnlp.1022.pdf)
2. Li et al 2023: [Learning Math Reasoning from Self-Sampled Correct and Partially-Correct Solutions](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=4D4TSJE6-K)

Questions:
* What metrics did you use for the QA datasets? many of them use more than exact match, e.g. HotpotQA uses F1.
* The discussion around ablations in 5.3 is unclear. Are the trends consistent across different models and datasets? 
* It is odd to have only considered up to 200 instances for constructing preference pairs. Why is this experiment limited to such a small maximum? The difference between e.g. 160 and 200 seems much less important than the difference between 200 and 1000 or 1000 and 5000. 
* How many total preference pairs do you end up training on? It would be helpful to include this number in the main body of the paper.
* How stable is this approach to different prompts for state evaluation? Did you experiment with other prompts?

Limitations:
yes

Rating:
6
